coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as the nucleation sites and interfacial regulator to suppress lithium dendrites for high-loading lithium-sulfur battery
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yin
Chen, Wei
Lei, Tianyu
Jiao, Yu
Wang, Hongbo
Wang, Xuepeng
Rao, Gaofeng
Wang, Xianfu
Chen, Bo
Xiong, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Lithium–sulfur battery is one of the most promising candidates to take over from the conventional lithium-ion batteries for the next-generation high energy storage devices. Although plausible advances have been made on the performances of the composite cathode with high sulfur loading, the development of compatible protection strategies for lithium anode is seriously lagging behind. Here we report a new strategy to suppress the dendrite growth in lithium-sulfur batteries with high sulfur loading by introducing graphene quantum dots into the electrolyte. The graphene quantum dots serve as the heterogeneous sites for uniform nucleation and provide continual regulation for the dendrite-free lithium deposition. The in-situ Raman spectroscopy reveals the enrichment of the GQDs at the electrode-electrolyte interface for the regulated electric field and ion flux, resulting in the dendrite-free Li deposition. As a result, the critical current of short circuit induced by lithium dendrite increases up to 7.44 mA cm−2, and the soft-short risk is excluded when cycling at the current density of 3 mA cm−2 with areal capacity of 3 mAh cm−2 for more than 500 h, demonstrating the excellent dendrite suppressing action of the GQDs. As a proof of concept, high-loading lithium-sulfur batteries using the GQDs-modified anolyte are fabricated with stable Coulombic efficiency of 99% at the current density of 3 mA cm−2 with sulfur loading of 4 mg cm−2 over 200 cycles. Our results provide a novel and facile approach to tackle the intrinsic problem on the lithium anode for high-loading lithium-sulfur batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Lithium metal
Anode protection
Lithium-sulfur batteries

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The absorption spectra of GQDs and GO. (b) The 2D fluorescence spectra of the GQDs. (c) The emission spectra of the GQDs excited under light with different wavelengths. (d) XRD patterns of the pristine graphite, graphene oxide and GQDs. (e) TEM image of the mono-dispersed GQDs and (f) the corresponding size distribution based on multiple images. (g) AFM image of the GQDs on the mica substrate and (h) corresponding height profile of the green line A-B. (i) The C 1s XPS spectrum of GQDs.

(a) Voltage-time curves of the Li|Li cells using electrolyte with and without GQDs cycled at current density of 3 mA cm−2 with the capacity of 3 mAh cm−2. Voltage-time curves of the Li|Li cells using electrolyte (b) with and (c) without GQDs at step-increased current densities. The stripping/plating performances of the Li|Cu cells (d) with and (e) without GQDs in the electrolyte at current density of 1 mA cm−2. (f) Cycling performances of the Li|Cu cells at the current density of 1 mA cm−2 with 1 mAh cm−2 plating capacity in each cycle.

SEM images of Li deposits in Li|Cu cells (a,b,e,f) with and (c,d,g,h) without GQDs in the electrolyte at the current density of 3 mA cm−2. The deposition capacities of lithium in (a–d) and (e–h) are 0.5 mAh cm−2 and 3 mAh cm−2, respectively. SEM images of the Li deposits in Li|Li cells operated at 3 mA cm−2 with 3 mAh cm−2 (i,j) with GQDs in the electrolyte for 500 h and (k,l) without GQDs in the electrolyte when the soft-short occurs. The scale bars in (a–h) are 10 μm. (m) Illustration of the GQDs regulated deposition processes.

In-situ Raman spectra of the electrolyte (a,b) without and (c,d) with GQDs. (e) The illustration of the in-situ Raman experiment in the quartz cuvette. The laser beam was focused in the electrolyte near the surface of the Cu foil.

CE comparison of the Li–S batteries with and without GQDs in the electrolyte cycled at (a) 0.5C, and (b) various rates. (c) Self-discharge profiles of the Li–S batteries after charging to 2.7 V. (d) CV curve of the Li–S batteries with GQDs in the electrolyte after cycling for 200 cycles at 0.5C and standing for 25 h. Inset is the CV curve of the cell without GQDs in the electrolyte. The voltage window is 1.7–2.7 V with the scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as the nucleation sites and interfacial regulator to suppress lithium dendrites for high-loading lithium-sulfur battery

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100029, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yin Hu, Wei Chen and Tianyu Lei contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium–sulfur battery is one of the most promising candidates to take over from the conventional lithium-ion batteries for the next-generation high energy storage devices. Although plausible advances have been made on the performances of the composite cathode with high sulfur loading, the development of compatible protection strategies for lithium anode is seriously lagging behind. Here we report a new strategy to suppress the dendrite growth in lithium-sulfur batteries with high sulfur loading by introducing graphene quantum dots into the electrolyte. The graphene quantum dots serve as the heterogeneous sites for uniform nucleation and provide continual regulation for the dendrite-free lithium deposition. The in-situ Raman spectroscopy reveals the enrichment of the GQDs at the electrode-electrolyte interface for the regulated electric field and ion flux, resulting in the dendrite-free Li deposition. As a result, the critical current of short circuit induced by lithium dendrite increases up to 7.44 mA cm−2, and the soft-short risk is excluded when cycling at the current density of 3 mA cm−2 with areal capacity of 3 mAh cm−2 for more than 500 h, demonstrating the excellent dendrite suppressing action of the GQDs. As a proof of concept, high-loading lithium-sulfur batteries using the GQDs-modified anolyte are fabricated with stable Coulombic efficiency of 99% at the current density of 3 mA cm−2 with sulfur loading of 4 mg cm−2 over 200 cycles. Our results provide a novel and facile approach to tackle the intrinsic problem on the lithium anode for high-loading lithium-sulfur batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Developments in portable electronic devices and vehicles arouses the demand to explore the next generation rechargeable batteries with high energy density, excellent rate capability and superior cycling stability. Till now, the status of the commercial lithium-ion batteries has not been shaken, owing to the long lifespan and controllable safety. However, limited by the host effect of the graphitic carbon anode, the energy density of the lithium-ion batteries is reaching the theoretical celling [1,2]. Therefore, new battery systems beyond lithium-ion batteries are urgently required to be designed and developed.

Lithium metal batteries (LMBs) including lithium-sulfur (Li–S) batteries and lithium-air (Li–O2) batteries exhibit high theoretical energy density of 2600 Wh Kg−1 and 3500 Wh kg−1, respectively, ten times higher than that of the LIBs (about ~350 Wh kg−1) [3]. However, due to the uncontrollable growth of the dendrites, gradually deteriorated electrochemical performances and terrible safety issues occur during the lithium plating/stripping process, hindering the commercialization of the LMBs [4]. The formation of dendrites is mainly attributed to the unevenly distributed electric field and Li ion flux at the electrode-electrolyte interface [5,6]. Moreover, the extended dendrites provide large active surface area for the reactions between the Li metal and electrolyte to form solid electrolyte interphases (SEI), consuming the active materials and electrolyte irreversibly [7]. In the worst scenario, the dendrites can impale the separator then contact with the cathode, causing short circuit of the batteries. Therefore, how to suppress the growth of lithium dendrite thus realize dendrite-free lithium anode is critical for the real application of LMBs.

Chazalviel space charge model is widely used for the description of the nucleation and early dendrite growth of lithium. As well known, the initial morphology of the Li nuclei plays a crucial role in the subsequent deposition and growth of lithium. However, the non-uniform surface of the current collector often leads to defect-induced nucleation and uncontrollable Li deposition. To tackle this issue, Liu et al. pre-planted Cu nanoparticles (Cu NPs) into the lithium foil and constructed multilayer structure through simple rolling and folding method [8]. The Cu NPs dispersed at the surface and interlayer of lithium plates serve as the heterogeneous seeds to effectively regulate the nucleation and growth of lithium. To further reveal the underlying mechanism of the guided lithium nucleation process, Zhang and colleagues demonstrate that the lithiophilic sites can reduce the deposition over potential of lithium nucleation, which is essential for inducing uniform deposition [9,10]. Inspired by co-deposition strategy in the conventional electroplating industry, Cheng et al. used nano-diamond (ND) as electrolyte additive to suppress the growth of Li dendrite [11]. The Li ions adsorbed on the surface of the ND particles were carried to the electrode surface to be reduced. Due to the low diffusion energy barrier of the adsorbed Li ions on the surface of the nano-diamond, uniform and dendrite-free lithium deposition is commonly achieved, leading to the enhanced electrochemical properties.

Compared to the strategies involving the construction of the anode host and the fabrication of the artificial SEI, the modification of the electrolyte is a promising method due to its simplicity towards scaling up for commercial applications. As the most commonly used electrolyte for Li–S batteries, ether-based electrolytes possess the advantages of moderate solvation for polysulfides and compatibility with the lithium metal anode [12]. Although tremendous efforts have been made to prevent the dendrite growth and enhance the performance of the LMBs by the modification of the electrolyte, the stable plating/stripping of Li can only be achieved under unpractical battery parameters (current density ≤2 mA cm−2 and areal capacity ≤2 mAh cm−2) [13–17]. For the Li–S batteries, much higher capacity of the Li anode is required as the capacity of the sulfur cathode is greatly larger than that of the conventional cathodes for LIBs [18–20]. Therefore, new strategies targeting the dendrite suppression of the lithium metal anode under high areal capacity conditions (≥3 mAh cm−2) need to be developed.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with the lateral size of a few nanometers, represent a new member of the carbon material family [21]. Due to the quantum confinement effect induced tunable electronic properties, the GQDs have been used as the active site to modulate the properties of the electrochemical interface [22–24]. Inspired by this, herein, by introducing GQDs into the electrolyte, uniform Li deposition and dendrite-free Li anode are realized for high-areal-capacity Li–S battery. The GQDs with average lateral size of ~2 nm and thickness of 1–3 layers serve as the heterogeneous seeds for the homogeneous and compact lithium nucleation. Moreover, combining the scanning electron microscopy studies and in situ Raman results, it is demonstrated that the well dispersed GQDs in the electrolyte continually regulate the ion distribution at the electrochemical interface and avoid the generated local electric field during the subsequent lithium deposition process, which benefit the uniform deposition and growth of lithium [25]. As a result, stable cycling capability of the lithium symmetry cell with large areal capacity of 3 mAh cm−2 was achieved using the GQDs based electrolyte at current density of 3 mA cm−2. Moreover, in-situ Raman tracking was carried out in lithium-copper asymmetry cell to uncover the nucleation and growth behaviors of the lithium deposit. Li–S batteries with high sulfur loading and high Coulombic efficiency was also achieved using the GQDs modified electrolyte. Our study provides a new strategy to suppress Li dendrite for high-areal-capacity LMBs.

GO from GP was synthesized using modified Hummer's method. The freeze-dried GO was quickly insert into a cube furnace at 350 °C under ambient atmosphere for several seconds until a subtle ‘spark’ reaction happened. This phenomenon can be explained by the sudden pressure release within the GO layers due to the removal of heated water and/or surface functional groups [26]. The sparked GO (300 mg) were added into a mixture of H2SO4 (3.2 M) and HNO3 (1.8 M) solution (30 mL in total) and refluxed for 10 h. After cooling down, 30 mL deionized water (DI) was added, and the pH was adjusted to 7 using KOH solution. The solution was then vacuum filtered through membrane (0.22 μm pore size) to get rid of the unbroken part of GO. The filtrate was cleaned via dialysis against fresh DI water for 48 h (tubing cut-off molecular weight ~1000). After removing the water by lyophilization, GQDs were obtained.

In Ar-filled glove box, 2 mg of the as-synthesized GQDs were added into Li bis(trifluoromethane)sulfonimide (LiTFSI, 1.0 M) with addition of 2.0 wt% LiNO3 dissolving in 4 mL 1,3-dioxolane (DOL) and 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME; 1:1 by volume) and stirred for 24 h 200 mg polyethylene oxide (Aladdin) was added into the electrolyte and the mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 24 h. The GQDs-containing electrolyte was obtained as gel like fluid with light yellow color. The preparation of the electrolyte without GQDs follows the same procedure except adding GQDs.

Symmetrical Li|Li and asymmetrical Li|Cu cells were assembled using 60 μL GQDs-containing electrolyte. For the Li–S and Li@Cu–S batteries, the C/S cathode was obtained by coating the mixture of sulfur, acetylene black and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in the ratio of 8:1:1 onto the carbon paper. The average sulfur loading is ≈ 4 mg cm−230 μL GQDs-containing electrolyte was dropped onto the Li anode, then covered by a Celgard 2500 separator, and 15 μL mgs−1 Li–S electrolyte without GQDs and PEO was dropped to the cathode side. All batteries were assembled using 2025 coin-type cell. Galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements were carried out on a LAND CT2001A within 1.7–2.7 V. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on electrochemical workstation (CHI 660A) in the frequency range of 0.01–105 Hz.

SEM (FEI NANOSEI 450) was used to observe the morphology of the lithium deposition. TEM was performed on the high-resolution FEI G20 system (USA). XPS was measured on Kratos-Axis Supra, the binding energy is calibrated with C1s = 284.5 eV. AFM was used to determine the thickness of the GQDs on SPI3800 N, Seiko (Tokyo, Japan). XRD measurements were carried out on a D/MAX-2000 PC, Rigaku, Ltd. with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å). Li|Cu cells in a sealed quartz cuvette was used for the in situ Raman analysis. The Renishaw InVia conocal micro-Raman was equipped with a 785 nm laser.

GQDs were prepared by a simple oxidation method involving chemical breakdown of the graphite oxide (GO), in which GO was obtained by modified Hummer's method using flake graphite as the source. The absorption and fluorescence characterizations were carried out to confirm the successful synthesis of the as-prepared GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, an extra absorption band is observed in the spectrum of GQDs compared to that of the GO, indicating the formation of the quantum sized graphene domains [27]. When excited under light with different wavelengths, the fluorescence peak shifts to longer wavelengths and its intensity decreases slightly along with the increase of the wavelengths, in accordance with the previous observations (Fig. 1b and c) [28]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the flake graphite, GO and GQDs are displayed in Fig. 1d. The characteristic (002) peak at 26.54 °C of the flack graphite disappears after the oxidation exfoliation, while a new peak at 10.76 corresponding to the (001) diffraction peak of GO is observed. The GO was treated at 350 °C in air for the expansion and was refluxed in the oxidizing acid environment for further breakdown. The diffraction peak of GO disappears for the as-synthesized GQDs sample, suggesting the successful exfoliation [29]. Fig. 1e shows the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the GO-derived GQDs. The size statistics of more than 200 dots from multiple images is shown in Fig. 1f, demonstrating the relatively narrow size distribution of 2.09 nm in average with 0.43 nm standard deviation. The atomic force microscope (AFM) image in Fig. 1g demonstrates the topographic morphology of the GQDs. The height profile of Line A-B displayed in Fig. 1h shows that the thickness of the GQDs is between 0.8 and 1.6 nm, corresponding to 1–3 graphene layers [28,30]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out to probe the composition of the GQDs (Fig. 1i). The C1s spectrum in Fig. 1i can be fitted and deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.5, 286.3 and 287.8 eV, corresponding to CC, C–O and CO bonds, respectively, indicating that the GQDs are functionalized with hydroxyl and carbonyl groups [30].

The as-synthesized GQDs were initially dispersed in the electrolyte containing Li bis(trifluoromethane)sulfonimide (LiTFSI) with addition of LiNO3 dissolving in 1,3-dioxolane (DOL) and 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME; 1:1 by volume) as shown in Fig. S1a. However, we found that the GQDs would precipitate after a few hours' stand (Fig. S1b). Therefore, the polyethylene oxide (PEO) was added to provide the gel-like framework to hold the GQDs (Fig. S1c), after which the GQDs can be suspended in the electrolyte without any change for one month (Fig. S1d). In this case, the dispersed GQDs can not only serve as the initial nucleation seeds, but also regulate the growth process of the deposited lithium continually. As a proof of concept, the galvanostatic cycling stability of Li metal electrodes was evaluated by Li|Li symmetrical cells using the electrolyte with and without GQDs as the additive. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the Li electrodes exhibit stable cycling performance in the GQDs added electrolyte for more than 500 h at the current density of 3 mA cm−2 and areal capacity of 3 mAh cm−2. The polarization is only about 20 mV, much lower than the reported values, indicating facilitated reaction kinetics [17,31]. By comparison, the cell using GQDs-free electrolyte shows sudden voltage drop after about 30 h, and maintains very low overpotential (less than 10 mV) thereafter (Fig. 2a and Fig. S2). This deceptively ‘good’ result of the cell using electrolyte without GQDs can be attributed to the internal short circuit induced by the wild growth of the lithium dendrite [32]. The nonzero voltage after short circuit comes from the nonzero resistance of the dendrites, which is called the ‘soft-short’ [33,34]. To monitor the electrochemical properties of the Li|Li cells during cycling, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) study was carried out and the results are shown in Fig. S3. All the Nyquist plots of the cells show well defined semicircles in the middle-frequency region during initial cycles, corresponding to charge-transfer resistance (R ct). However, when the soft-short occurs in the cell using electrolyte without GQDs, the impedance of the cell becomes immeasurable. The galvanostatic cycling of the Li|Li cells with charge/discharge time of 1 h at step-increased current densities (0.12 mA cm−2 per step) was carried out to determine the critical current density (at which short circuit occurs induced by lithium dendrite). After cycling for a certain amount of time, all of the cells (with and without GQDs additive) suffered a voltage drop, which can be ascribed to the formation of lithium dendrite. The critical current density for the cell using electrolyte with GQDs is determined to be 7.44 mA cm−2 (Fig. 2b), much higher than that of the cell using electrolyte without GQDs (5.88 mA cm−2, Fig. 2c), demonstrating the improved dendrite-suppressing capability of the electrolyte with GQDs.

The Li|Cu asymmetry cells were assembled to evaluate the plating/stripping reversibility of lithium in the electrolyte with and without GQDs. For each cycle, a certain amount of Li is plated onto the Cu electrode. Subsequently, the Li is allowed to be stripped at the same current density until the voltage of the cell rises to 1 V, which enables the complete Li stripping from the Cu electrode. The Coulombic efficiency (CE) of the Li|Cu asymmetry cells using electrolyte with and without GQDs are compared when cycled at 1 mA cm−2 with different total capacities as displayed in Fig. 2d and e, respectively. Clearly, the cell based on GQDs-modified electrolyte can be operated at high areal capacity up to 5 mAh cm−2 (plating and stripping at 1 mA cm−2 for 5 h) with the Coulombic efficiency (CE) close to 100%. By comparison, the cell using electrolyte without GQDs shows dramatically fluctuating CE. The rate performances of the Li|Cu asymmetry cells are also evaluated by fixing the plating and stripping time to 1 h while varying the current density. As shown in Fig. S4, compared to the cell using electrolyte without GQDs, the cell based on electrolyte with GQDs shows much improved rate performance even at the high current density of 4 mA cm−2, indicating the crucial role of the GQDs for regulating the electrochemical processes. The prolonged cycling reversibility of the Li|Cu asymmetry cells is further evaluated at 1 mA cm−2 with the areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2. The CE of the cell with GQDs in the electrolyte maintains at about 99% for 200 cycles. In comparison, the CE of the cell without GQDs in the electrolyte is fluctuating greatly and decreasing gradually along with the cycling. In addition, to eliminate the possibility that PEO may contribute to the lithium deposition, the performance of the original electrolyte without PEO and GQDs is evaluated (Fig. S5). The striking contrast suggests that the GQDs in the electrolyte can significantly promote the reversibility of lithium cycling.

The geometries of the Li deposits on the Cu foil is studied using scanning electron microscope (SEM) to clarify the role of GQDs on the nucleation and growth of plated lithium. Fig. 3 a–d displays the morphology of the Li deposits after the initial nucleation process (discharged at 3 mA cm−2 to reach a capacity of 0.5 mAh cm−2). Obviously, the compact and uniform nucleation morphology is achieved in the GQDs based electrolyte, as shown in Fig. 3a and b. By comparison, without GQDs in the electrolyte, an uneven nucleation morphology is shown, where aggregation and vacancy already existed before the growth process (Fig. 3c and d). These results demonstrate the GQDs can serve as the nucleation sites to guide the initial lithium plating, which is crucial to inhibit the self-amplified growth of dendrites. The nuclei size in the electrolyte with GQDs is 1.52 μm, much larger than that in the electrolyte without GQDs (1.1 μm), indicating that the well dispersed GQDs in the electrolyte induce uniform local current density at the electrochemical interface, because it is argued that the Li ions in the electrolyte would prefer to deposit on the “hot spots” of the substrate, leading to the elevated local current density [35,36] and reduced nucleation size [37]. The subsequent morphologies of the deposits with a total plating capacity of 3 mAh cm−2 are shown in Fig. 3 e-h. A uniform columnar structure without dendrite was obtained (Fig. 3e) with thickness of 15.5 μm (Fig. S6) and diameter of 3.2 μm (Fig. 3f) in the GQDs-modified electrolyte. On the contrary, the Li growth in the electrolyte without GQDs is more random as large-size and irregular deposits were formed on the Cu electrode (Fig. 3g and h). SEM images of the cycled electrodes from the Li|Li cells operated at the current density of 3 mA cm−2 with the capacity of 3 mAh cm−2 after 500 h also reveal a uniform and dendrite-free morphology in the GQDs-containing electrolyte (Fig. 3i and j), compared to the large amount of dendrite in the GQDs-free electrolyte (Fig. 3k and l). These results further evidence the regulating effect of the GQDs in the electrolyte for the even growth of the deposited lithium. Therefore, the suppression for lithium dendrite in the GQDs-modified electrolyte can be concluded in Fig. 3m. It is demonstrated that the Li ions show enhanced diffusion along the graphene edges due to the decreased diffusion barrier [38]. Correspondingly, the GQDs synthesized from chemical breakdown of RGO possess abundant edge sites, where the Li ions can diffuse easily and distribute uniformly during the deposition process. As the result, the GQDs dispersed in the electrolyte can serves as a regulator to continually regulate the Li flux at the electrode-electrolyte interface and ensure the dendrite-free growth. While without GQDs in the electrolyte, the hot spots on the surface of the current collector causes enlarged local spatial charges and uneven ion flux, leading to unstable dendritic growth.

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool to probe the polarizable surface in vibrational energy levels. Therefore, to reveal the mechanism of the GQDs-regulated Li deposition, in-situ Raman spectroscopy was carried out in a Li|Cu cell. The laser beam was set parallel to the surface of Cu foil and was focused near the surface of the Cu foil towards the Li metal side. The operando Raman spectra measured during the Li plating processes at the current density of 3 mA cm−2 with total capacity of 3 mAh cm−2 are shown in Fig. 4 . It should be noted that at the beginning of the plating, the Raman signals shows the original state of the bulk solution. When the thickness of the plated Li grows, the electrochemical interface moves towards the light spot, where depletion of the ions can be detected. Before the electrochemical plating process, the Raman spectrum of the electrolyte without GQDs show featured band at 741 cm−1 and 942 cm−1, corresponding to the S–N stretching in coordinated TFSI− and Li-coordinated solvent band, respectively (Fig. 4a–b) [16]. As the deposition proceed, the intensities of the above two bands decreases dramatically, demonstrating the depletion of the lithium ions at the electrochemical interface. According to the dendrite growth model proposed by Chazalviel, the concentration of lithium ion in the vicinity of the negative electrode will drop to zero at Sand's time, resulting in excess of spatial charges at the electrochemical interface [34]. The hot spots on the surface of the current collector causes enlarged local spatial charges and uneven ion flux, leading to unstable dendritic growth. For the cell using GQDs-modified electrolyte, in addition to the bands at 741 cm−1 and 942 cm−1, two new bands at ~1350 cm−1 and 1535 cm−1 appear in the Raman spectra after 20 min' plating (Fig. 4 c and d), which can be assigned to the D and G bands of the GQDs (Fig. S7) [30]. The observation can be rationalized by an illustration depicted in Fig. 4e. When the Li+ depletion is observed, the accumulated GQDs are also observed at the electrochemical interfaces, serving as a regulator to continually smooth the local electrical field and ensure even ion flux near the electrochemical interface, resulting in dendrite-free the deposition. Beside the accumulation of GQDs, the enrichment of the coordinated TFSI− near the electrochemical interface in the GQDs-modified electrolyte can also be identified, which is demonstrated by the enhanced intensity of the band at 741 cm−1 relative to the intensity of Li-coordinated solvent band at 942 cm−1. The regulated Li+ distribution can offset the negative effects induced by the ion barren region, thus leading to the uniform deposition [25]. Combining the morphology characterization from SEM and the in situ Raman study, it is demonstrated that the dispersed GQDs not only serve the sites for the guided nucleation, but also continually regulate the Li+ deposition behavior at the electrochemical interface during plating, resulting in the promoted cycling stability of the lithium anode at high current density with high deposition capacity.

Encouraged by the effective dendrite suppression by GQDs, we proceeded to assemble the Li–S batteries using lithium as the anode, carbon/sulfur (C/S) composite as the cathode and GQDs-modified electrolyte as the anolyte. The sulfur loading of the C/S cathode is ~4 mg cm−2. As shown in Fig. 5 a, after being activated at 0.1C for 5 cycles, the Li–S battery with GQDs-added electrolyte shows stable cycling Coulombic efficiency (CE) of ≈99% at 0.5C (≈3 mA cm−2), delivering an initial capacity of 830 mAh g−1 (≈3.32 mAh cm−2) with 0.2% decay per cycle for 200 cycles (Fig. S8). For comparison, the Li–S battery using electrolyte without GQDs can cycle normally for 40 cycles at 0.5C, followed by a dramatic decrease of the CE. The deterioration behavior of the Li–S battery is in accordance with that of the Li|Li cell, which is caused by the soft short resulted from the lithium dendrites. The rate performance of the Li–S batteries is also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5b, the battery with GQDs-modified electrolyte shows stable CE above 99% when the discharge/charge rate gradually increases from 0.1C to 1C, corresponding to an initial discharge capacity of 984 mAh g−1 (~4.0 mAh cm−2) at 0.1C and 435 mAh g−1 (1.7 mAh cm−2) at 1C (Fig. S9). Moreover, when the rate switched back to 0.5C, the battery still shows reversible capacities. However, without the anode protection from GQDs, the Li–S battery shows deteriorative CE as the rate increases. Furthermore, after switching back to 0.5C, the battery shows irreversible capacity with significantly dropped CE, which can also be attributed to the lithium dendrite caused soft-short. To further compare the internal soft-short in the batteries, self-discharge was tested when the batteries were stopped at 2.7 V after 200 discharge/charge cycles at 0.5C. As shown in Fig. 5c, the voltage of the Li–S battery with GQDs in the electrolyte keeps constant at the equilibrium potential of 2.33 V for more than 20 h, compared to the continually decreased voltage of the Li–S battery without GQDs in the electrolyte, indicating the complete elimination of the soft-short peril. Fig. 5d shows the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the Li–S batteries with and without GQDs in the electrolyte at 0.1 mV s−1 after standing at open voltage. Clearly, the battery with GQDs-based electrolyte shows good redox with two distinct cathodic peaks and one anodic peak. By comparison, the redox behaviors of the sulfur species completely disappear in the Li–S battery without GQDs in the electrolyte, and the currents are extremely high due to the internal soft-short (inset in Fig. 5d), suggesting the crucial role of the GQDs to suppress Li dendrites in Li–S battery.

The use of thick Li foil anodes with large excessive capacity (~100 mAh cm−2) in Li–S batteries often results in compromised energy density [39]. To realize high-energy-density Li–S batteries, Li@Cu electrode was fabricated by plating lithium with capacity of 4.5 mAh cm−2 on Cu foil and was used as anode for Li–S batteries. As shown in Fig. S10, with a sulfur loading of 4 mg cm−2 in the cathode, the CE of the Li@Cu–S battery without GQDs drops after ~150 cycles, indicating the soft-short occurs in the battery. Excitingly, the as-assembled Li@Cu–S battery with GQDs-based electrolyte can deliver initial capacity of 2.3 mAh cm−2 at 0.4C with CE higher than 99%, promising for the next-generation high-energy-density devices.

In summary, our work presents a simple and practical strategy to suppress the lithium dendrite by introducing GQDs into the electrolyte, which can serve as the seeds at the electrode surface to induce dense and uniform lithium nucleation due to the regulated electrical field and ion flux near the electrode-electrolyte interface. In-situ Raman study confirms the crucial role of the GQDs for the continual regulation of the lithium growth process, leading to the dendrite-free deposition. As a result, the critical current of the internal short circuit induced by the lithium dendrite increases from 5.88 to 7.44 mA cm−2 after GQDs incorporation, and the soft-short in the battery with the practical areal capacity of 3 mAh cm−2 is eliminated at the current density of 3 mA cm−2. As a proof of concept, the high-loading lithium-sulfur batteries with high Coulombic efficiency are achieved using the anolyte with GQDs for the anode protection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
